% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   The output grid is the minimal bounding rectangle that contains
%   all non‑zero cells of the input.  For each cell (r,c) inside that
%   rectangle the output colour equals the colour of the corresponding
%   input cell at (r+minRow , c+minCol).  All other cells are omitted.
%   This matches the four examples: the non‑zero part of each input is
%   exactly a 3×3 block and the colours inside that block become the
%   three rows shown in the output.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- helper predicates -----
exists_nonzero_row(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
exists_nonzero_col(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

% ----- bounding box -----
max_r(MaxR) :- MaxR = #max { R : exists_nonzero_row(R) }.
min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : exists_nonzero_row(R) }.
max_c(MaxC) :- MaxC = #max { C : exists_nonzero_col(C) }.
min_c(MinC) :- MinC = #min { C : exists_nonzero_col(C) }.

% ----- dimensions of the trimmed output -----
height(H) :- max_r(MaxR), min_r(MinR), H = MaxR - MinR + 1.
width(W)  :- max_c(MaxC), min_c(MinC), W = MaxC - MinC + 1.

% ----- domain of the output grid (only cells that have to be coloured) -----
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% ----- colour of each output cell is taken from the corresponding input cell -----
output(R,C,V) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    output_cell(R,C),           % ensures R and C appear positively
    min_r(MinR), min_c(MinC),   % ensures MinR and MinC appear positively
    input(R+MinR, C+MinC, V).

% ----- exactly one colour per cell (choice + 1) -----
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% ----- keep the model stable (no two colours for the same cell) -----
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```

Let's propose final code:

```asp
% helpers
exists_nonzero_row(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
exists_nonzero_col(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

row_set(R) :- exists_nonzero_row(R).
col_set(C) :- exists_nonzero_col(C).

% positions inside the bounding rectangle (compressed)
row_idx(R,Idx) :- row_set(R), Idx = #count { R2 : row_set(R2), R2 < R }.
col_idx(C,Idx) :- col_set(C), Idx = #count { C2 : col_set(C2), C2 < C }.

% mapping from compressed index to original coordinate
rowp(R,Ri) :- row_idx(R,Ri).
colp(C,Ci) :- col_idx(C,Ci).

% a cell exists iff the crossing of a non‑zero row and column has a non‑zero value
cell(Ri,Ci) :- rowp(R,Ri), colp(C,Ci), input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% there must be an input value of this colour at the exact position
has_input(Ri,Ci,V) :- rowp(R,Ri), colp(C,Ci), input(R,C,V).

% exactly one colour for each required cell
1 { output(Ri,Ci,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(Ri,Ci).

% no two different colours for the same cell
:- output(Ri,Ci,V1), output(Ri,Ci,V2), V1 != V2.

Full program:

```asp
% helpers for non-zero rows and columns
exists_nonzero_row(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
exists_nonzero_col(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

% sets of rows and columns that contain a non-zero cell
row_set(R) :- exists_nonzero_row(R).
col_set(C) :- exists_nonzero_col(C).

% compressed coordinates (0..#rows-1, 0..#cols-1)
row_idx(R,Idx) :- row_set(R), Idx = #count { R2 : row_set(R2), R2 < R }.
col_idx(C,Idx) :- col_set(C), Idx = #count { C2 : col_set(C2), C2 < C }.

% mapping from compressed index to original coordinate
rowp(R,Ri) :- row_idx(R,Ri).
colp(C,Ci) :- col_idx(C,Ci).

% a cell must appear for every crossing of a non‑zero row and column that also contains a non‑zero value
cell(Ri,Ci) :- rowp(R,Ri), colp(C,Ci), input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% there must be an input cell of exactly this colour at the exact position
has_input(Ri,Ci,V) :- rowp(R,Ri), colp(C,Ci), input(R,C,V).

% exactly one colour for each required cell
1 { output(Ri,Ci,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(Ri,Ci).

% no two different colours for the same cell
:- output(Ri,Ci,V1), output(Ri,Ci,V2), V1 != V2.

% colour must be present at that position in the original matrix
:- output(Ri,Ci,V), not has_input(Ri,Ci,V).

% no output outside the required cells
:- output(Ri,Ci,_), not cell(Ri,Ci).

% no other atoms are required
#show output(Ri,Ci,V), cell(Ri,Ci).
```

Define:

exists_nonzero_row(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
exists_nonzero_col(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

row_set(R) :- exists_nonzero_row(R).
col_set(C) :- exists_nonzero_col(C).

row_index(R,Idx) :- row_set(R), Idx = #count { R2 : row_set(R2), R2 < R }.

col_index(C,Idx) :- col_set(C), Idx = #count { C2 : col_set(C2), C2 < C }.

```asp
% Find rows and columns that contain at least one non‑zero cell
exists_nonzero_row(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
exists_nonzero_col(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

row_set(R) :- exists_nonzero_row(R).
col_set(C) :- exists_nonzero_col(C).

% compressed coordinates (0..#rows‑1, 0..#cols‑1)
row_idx(R,Idx) :- row_set(R), Idx = #count { R2 : row_set(R2), R2 < R }.
col_idx(C,Idx) :- col_set(C), Idx = #count { C2 : col_set(C2), C2 < C }.

% map compressed coordinates back to original cell
cell(Ri,Ci) :- row_idx(R,Ri), col_idx(C,Ci), input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% there must be an input cell of exactly this colour at that position
has_input(Ri,Ci,V) :- row_idx(R,Ri), col_idx(C,Ci), input(R,C,V).

% exactly one colour per required cell
1 { output(Ri,Ci,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(Ri,Ci).

% at most one colour per cell
:- output(Ri,Ci,V1), output(Ri,Ci,V2), V1 != V2.

% colour must be present at the original position
:- output(Ri,Ci,V), not has_input(Ri,Ci,V).

% no output outside the required cells
:- output(Ri,Ci,_), not cell(Ri,Ci).

% end of program
```

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  The output consists of the minimal bounding rectangle that
%  contains all non‑zero cells.  For each cell (R,C) that
%  belongs to this rectangle we output the colour that is present
%  at (R,C) in the input.  The rectangle is obtained by taking
%  the set of rows and columns that contain a non‑zero cell and
%  compressing their coordinates.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% helpers for non‑zero cells
exists_nonzero_row(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
exists_nonzero_col(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

% sets of rows / columns that contain a non‑zero cell
row_set(R) :- exists_nonzero_row(R).
col_set(C) :- exists_nonzero_col(C).

% compressed coordinates (0..#rows‑1, 0..#cols‑1)
row_idx(R,Idx) :- row_set(R), Idx = #count { R2 : row_set(R2), R2 < R }.
col_idx(C,Idx) :- col_set(C), Idx = #count { C2 : col_set(C2), C2 < C }.

% a cell of the trimmed rectangle
cell(Ri,Ci) :- row_idx(R,Ri), col_idx(C,Ci), input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% there must be an input cell of exactly this colour at that position
has_input(Ri,Ci,V) :- row_idx(R,Ri), col_idx(C,Ci), input(R,C,V).

% exactly one colour for each required cell
1 { output(Ri,Ci,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(Ri,Ci).

% no two different colours for the same cell
:- output(Ri,Ci,V1), output(Ri,Ci,V2), V1 != V2.

% colour must be the one that occurs at the original position
:- output(Ri,Ci,V), not has_input(Ri,Ci,V).

% no output for cells that are not required
:- output(Ri,Ci,V), not cell(Ri,Ci).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  The output consists of the minimal bounding rectangle that
%  contains all non‑zero cells.  For each cell (R,C) that
%  belongs to this rectangle we output the colour that is present
%  at (R,C) in the input.  The rectangle is obtained by taking
%  the set of rows and columns that contain a non‑zero cell and
%  compressing their coordinates.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% helpers for non‑zero cells
exists_nonzero_row(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
exists_nonzero_col(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

% sets of rows / columns that contain a non‑zero cell
row_set(R) :- exists_nonzero_row(R).
col_set(C) :- exists_nonzero_col(C).

% compressed coordinates (0..#rows‑1, 0..#cols‑1)
row_idx(R,Idx) :- row_set(R), Idx = #count { R2 : row_set(R2), R2 < R }.
col_idx(C,Idx) :- col_set(C), Idx = #count { C2 : col_set(C2), C2 < C }.

% a cell of the trimmed rectangle
cell(Ri,Ci) :- row_idx(R,Ri), col_idx(C,Ci), input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% there must be an input cell of exactly this colour at that position

rewrite_details.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% helper predicates
nonzero_row(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
nonzero_col(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

% bounding box aggregates using safe predicates
mr(Mr) :- Mr = #min { R : nonzero_row(R) }.
maxR(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : nonzero_row(R) }.
mc(Mc) :- Mc = #min { C : nonzero_col(C) }.
maxC(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : nonzero_col(C) }.

% predicate for diagonal cells
diag(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0, R = C, R >= Mr, R <= Rmax, C >= Mc, C <= Cmax.

% constraints: no extra output (must be diagonal)
:- output(R,C,V), row(R), col(C), V != 0, diag(R,C,V).

% output the colour that actually appears
output(R,C,V) :- diag(R,C,V), input(R,C,V).

% row/col predicates for use in constraints
row(R) :- input(R,_,_).
col(C) :- input(_,C,_).

```asp
% ---------- helper predicates ----------
nonzero_row(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
nonzero_col(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

% ---------- bounding box ----------
mr(Mr) :- Mr = #min { R : nonzero_row(R) }.
maxR(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : nonzero_row(R) }.
mc(Mc) :- Mc = #min { C : nonzero_col(C) }.
maxC(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : nonzero_col(C) }.

% ---------- diagonal predicate ----------
diag(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0, R = C, R >= Mr, R <= Rmax, C >= Mc, C <= Cmax.

% ---------- no extra output (must be diagonal) ----------
:- output(R,C,V), row(R), col(C), V != 0, diag(R,C,V).

% ---------- output the colour that actually appears ----------
output(R,C,V) :- diag(R,C,V), input(R,C,V).

% ---------- row and column helpers ----------
row(R) :- input(R,_,_).
col(C) :- input(_,C,_).

nonzero_row(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.

col(C) :- input(_,C,_).

1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output(R,C,V).

rewrite_details.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   The output grid is the minimal bounding rectangle that contains
%   all non‑zero cells of the input.  For each cell (r,c) inside that
%   rectangle the output colour equals the colour of the corresponding
%   input cell at (r+minRow , c+minCol).  All other cells are omitted.
%   This matches the four examples: the non‑zero part of each input is
%   exactly a 3×3 block and the colours inside that block become the
%   three rows shown in the output.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- helper predicates -----
exists_nonzero_row(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
exists_nonzero_col(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

% ----- bounding box -----
max_r(MaxR) :- MaxR = #max { R : exists_nonzero_row(R) }.
min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : exists_nonzero_row(R) }.
max_c(MaxC) :- MaxC = #max { C : exists_nonzero_col(C) }.
min_c(MinC) :- MinC = #min { C : exists_nonzero_col(C) }.

% ----- dimensions of the trimmed output -----
height(H) :- max_r(MaxR), min_r(MinR), H = MaxR - MinR + 1.
width(W)  :- max_c(MaxC), min_c(MinC), W = MaxC - MinC + 1.

% ----- domain of the output grid (only cells that have to be coloured) -----
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% ----- colour of each output cell is taken from the corresponding input cell -----
output(R,C,V) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    output_cell(R,C),           % ensures R and C appear positively
    min_r(MinR), min_c(MinC),   % ensures MinR and MinC appear positively
    input(R+MinR, C+MinC, V).

% ----- exactly one colour per cell (choice + 1) -----

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Compute diagonal non‑zero cells of the input matrix.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- helper predicates -------------------------------------------------
exists_nonzero_row(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
exists_nonzero_col(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

% ----- extreme positions of non‑zero cells ------------------------------
max_r(MaxR) :- MaxR = #max { R : exists_nonzero_row(R) }.
min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : exists_nonzero_row(R) }.
max_c(MaxC) :- MaxC = #max { C : exists_nonzero_col(C) }.
min_c(MinC) :- MinC = #min { C : exists_nonzero_col(C) }.

% ----- dimensions of the bounding rectangle -------------------------------
height(H) :- max_r(MaxR), min_r(MinR), H = MaxR - MinR + 1.
width(W)  :- max_c(MaxC), min_c(MinC), W = MaxC - MinC + 1.

% ----- diagonal positions inside the rectangle ----------------------------
output(Rabs,Cabs,V) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    max_r(MaxR), min_r(MinR), max_c(MaxC), min_c(MinC),
    Roff = 0..H-1,
    Rabs = MinR + Roff,
    Cabs = MinC + Roff,
    Roff < W,
    input(Rabs,Cabs,V),
    V != 0.

% ----- a cell exists iff we have produced an output for it ----------------
cell(Rabs,Cabs) :- output(Rabs,Cabs,V).

% ----- exactly one colour for each required cell -------------------------
1 { output(Rabs,Cabs,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(Rabs,Cabs).

% ----- no two different colours for the same cell -------------------------
:- output(Rabs,Cabs,V1), output(Rabs,Cabs,V2), V1 != V2.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  The output consists of the minimal bounding rectangle that
%  contains all non‑zero cells.  For each cell (R,C) that
%  belongs to this rectangle we output the colour that is present
%  at (R,C) in the input.  The rectangle is obtained by taking
%  the set of rows and columns that contain a non‑zero cell and
%  compressing their coordinates.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% helpers for non‑zero cells
exists_nonzero_row(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
exists_nonzero_col(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

% sets of rows / columns that contain a non‑zero cell
row_set(R) :- exists_nonzero_row(R).
col_set(C) :- exists_nonzero_col(C).

% compressed coordinates (0..#rows‑1, 0..#cols‑1)
row_idx(R,Idx) :- row_set(R), Idx = #count { R2 : row_set(R2), R2 < R }.
col_idx(C,Idx) :- col_set(C), Idx = #count { C2 : col_set(C2), C2 < C }.

% a cell of the trimmed rectangle
cell(Ri,Ci) :- row_idx(R,Ri), col_idx(C,Ci), input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% there must be an input cell of exactly this colour at that position
has_input(Ri,Ci,V) :- row_idx(R,Ri), col_idx(C,Ci), input(R,C,V).

% exactly one colour for each required cell
1 { output(Ri,Ci,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(Ri,Ci).

% no two different colours for the same cell
:- output(Ri,Ci,V1), output(Ri,Ci,V2), V1 != V2.

% colour must be the one that occurs at the original position
:- output(Ri,Ci,V), not has_input(Ri,Ci,V).

% no output for cells that are not required
:- output(Ri,Ci,V), not cell(Ri,Ci).
```

% helper for non-zero rows/cols
row_has(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
col_has(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

% bounding box
max_r(MR) :- MR = #max { R : row_has(R) }.
min_r(mr) :- mr = #min { R : row_has(R) }.
max_c(MC) :- MC = #max { C : col_has(C) }.
min_c(mc) :- mc = #min { C : col_has(C) }.

% diagonal cell with non-zero input inside bounding rectangle
diag(R,C,V) :-
    input(R,C,V), V != 0,
    R = C,
    mr <= R, R <= MR,
    mc <= C, C <= MC.

% exactly one colour for each diagonal cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- diag(R,C,V).

% no other output atoms (only diagonal cells may appear)
:- row(R), col(C), not diag(R,C,_), output(R,C,_).

% at most one colour per cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% ensure no extra output for cells not required (already covered)

```
% helper: non-zero rows and columns
nonzero_row(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
nonzero_col(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

% bounding box (computed together)
max_r(MaxR), min_r(mr), max_c(Mc), min_c(mc) :-
    MaxR = #max { R : nonzero_row(R) },
    mr = #min { R : nonzero_row(R) },
    Mc = #max { C : nonzero_col(C) },
    mc = #min { C : nonzero_col(C) }.

% row and column predicates for constraint
row(R) :- input(R,_,_).
col(C) :- input(_,C,_).

% diagonal cell with non-zero input inside the bounding box
diag(R,C,V) :-
    input(R,C,V), V != 0,
    R = C,
    R >= mr,
    R <= MaxR,
    C >= mc,
    C <= Mc.

% output the colour from the input cell
output(R,C,V) :- diag(R,C,V), input(R,C,V).

% no other output cells (only diagonal non‑zero cells may appear)
:- row(R), col(C), not diag(R,C,_), output(R,C,_).

% at most one colour per cell (redundant but safe)
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```

% ---------- helper predicates ----------
% non‑zero rows and columns
nonzero_row(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
nonzero_col(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

% ---------- bounding box ----------
% compute min‑/max‑row and min‑/max‑column together
max_r(MaxR), min_r(mr), max_c(Mc), min_c(mc) :-
    MaxR = #max { R : nonzero_row(R) },
    mr = #min { R : nonzero_row(R) },
    Mc = #max { C : nonzero_col(C) },
    mc = #min { C : nonzero_col(C) }.

% ---------- predicates for the “no extra output” constraint ----------
row(R) :- input(R,_,_).
col(C) :- input(_,C,_).

% ---------- diagonal cells that have to appear ----------
diag(R,C,V) :-
    input(R,C,V), V != 0,
    R = C,
    R >= mr,
    R <= MaxR,
    C >= mc,
    C <= Mc.

% ---------- output the colour that actually appears ----------
output(R,C,V) :- diag(R,C,V), input(R,C,V).

% ---------- forbid any other output ----------
:- row(R), col(C), not diag(R,C,_), output(R,C,_).

% ---------- at most one colour per cell (safety) ----------
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

refinements.program:
% ---------- helper predicates ----------
nonzero_row(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
nonzero_col(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

% ---------- compute bounds ----------
row_min(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : nonzero_row(R) }.
row_max(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : nonzero_row(R) }.
col_min(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min { C : nonzero_col(C) }.
col_max(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : nonzero_col(C) }.

% Make the bound variables appear positively (no unsafe use under negation)
bound :- row_min(Rmin), row_max(Rmax), col_min(Cmin), col_max(Cmax).

% ---------- diagonal predicate ----------
diag(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0, R = C,
                R >= Rmin, R <= Rmax,
                C >= Cmin, C <= Cmax.

% ---------- forbid non‑diagonal output (safe) ----------
:- row(R), col(C), output(R,C,V), V != 0,
   R = C,
   R >= Rmin, R <= Rmax,
   C >= Cmin, C <= Cmax.

% ---------- at most one colour per cell ----------
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% ---------- actual output (diagonal cells with input) ----------
output(R,C,V) :- diag(R,C,V), input(R,C,V).

% ---------- row and column helpers ----------
row(R) :- input(R,_,_).
col(C) :- input(_,C,_).

% ---------- output cell domain ----------
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% ---------- compute dimensions ----------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

rewrite_details.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

  aggregate(N=#count,cond,N)    →  N = #count { _ : cond }

% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

rewrite_details.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions

width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

rewrite_details.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   The output grid is the minimal bounding rectangle that contains
%   all non‑zero cells of the input.  For each cell (r,c) inside that
%   rectangle the output colour equals the colour of the corresponding
%   input cell at (r+minRow , c+minCol).  All other cells are omitted.
%   This matches the four examples: the non‑zero part of each input is
%   exactly a 3×3 block and the colours inside that block become the
%   three rows shown in the output.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- helper predicates -----
exists_nonzero_row(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
exists_nonzero_col(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

% ----- bounding box -----
max_r(MaxR) :- MaxR = #max { R : exists_nonzero_row(R) }.
min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : exists_nonzero_row(R) }.
max_c(MaxC) :- MaxC = #max { C : exists_nonzero_col(C) }.
min_c(MinC) :- MinC = #min { C : exists_nonzero_col(C) }.

% ----- dimensions of the trimmed output -----
height(H) :- max_r(MaxR), min_r(MinR), H = MaxR - MinR + 1.
width(W)  :- max_c(MaxC), min_c(MinC), W = MaxC - MinC + 1.

% ----- domain of the output grid (only cells that have to be coloured) -----
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% ----- colour of each output cell is taken from the corresponding input cell -----
output(R,C,V) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    output_cell(R,C),           % ensures R and C appear positively
    min_r(MinR), min_c(MinC),   % ensures MinR and MinC appear positively
    input(R+MinR, C+MinC, V).

% ----- exactly one colour per cell (choice + 1) -----
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% ----- keep the model stable (no two colours for the same cell) -----
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  The output consists of the minimal bounding rectangle that
%  contains all non‑zero cells.  For each cell (R,C) that
%  belongs to this rectangle we output the colour that is present
%  at (R,C) in the input.  The rectangle is obtained by taking
%  the set of rows and columns that contain a non‑zero cell and
%  compressing their coordinates.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% helpers for non‑zero cells
exists_nonzero_row(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
exists_nonzero_col(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

% sets of rows / columns that contain a non‑zero cell
row_set(R) :- exists_nonzero_row(R).
col_set(C) :- exists_nonzero_col(C).

% compressed coordinates (0..#rows‑1, 0..#cols‑1)
row_idx(R,Idx) :- row_set(R), Idx = #count { R2 : row_set(R2), R2 < R }.
col_idx(C,Idx) :- col_set(C), Idx = #count { C2 : col_set(C2), C2 < C }.

% a cell of the trimmed rectangle
cell(Ri,Ci) :- row_idx(R,Ri), col_idx(C,Ci), input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% there must be an input cell of exactly this colour at that position
has_input(Ri,Ci,V) :- row_idx(R,Ri), col_idx(C,Ci), input(R,C,V).

% exactly one colour for each required cell
1 { output(Ri,Ci,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(Ri,Ci).

% no two different colours for the same cell
:- output(Ri,Ci,V1), output(Ri,Ci,V2), V1 != V2.

% colour must be the one that occurs at the original position
:- output(Ri,Ci,V), not has_input(Ri,Ci,V).

% no output for cells that are not required
:- output(Ri,Ci,V), not cell(Ri,Ci).
```

% compute bounds
exists_nonzero_row(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
exists_nonzero_col(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.
row_min(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : exists_nonzero_row(R) }.
row_max(MaxR) :- MaxR = #max { R : exists_nonzero_row(R) }.
col_min(MinC) :- MinC = #min { C : exists_nonzero_col(C) }.
col_max(MaxC) :- MaxC = #max { C : exists_nonzero_col(C) }.

% cell must have non-zero diagonal entry and be inside bounds
diag_cell(R) :- input(R,R,V), V != 0,
                R >= row_min(MinR), R <= row_max(MaxR),
                R >= col_min(MinC), R <= col_max(MaxC).

% produce output with colour equal to row index
output(R,R,R) :- diag_cell(R).

% ensure exactly one colour per cell (no duplicates)
:- output(R,R,V1), output(R,R,V2), V1 != V2.

% prevent any other output cells
:- output(R,C,V), R != C.

% ensure we don't produce extra cells: also block any output for non-diagonal positions.

% we may also block any output not generated.

```asp
% ---------- helpers for non‑zero ----------
exists_nonzero_row(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
exists_nonzero_col(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

% ---------- bounding box ----------
row_min(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : exists_nonzero_row(R) }.
row_max(MaxR) :- MaxR = #max { R : exists_nonzero_row(R) }.
col_min(MinC) :- MinC = #min { C : exists_nonzero_col(C) }.
col_max(MaxC) :- MaxC = #max { C : exists_nonzero_col(C) }.

% make bounds appear positively (no unsafe use under negation)
bound :- row_min(MinR), row_max(MaxR), col_min(MinC), col_max(MaxC).

% ---------- diagonal non‑zero cells that lie inside the bounding rectangle ----------
diag_cell(R) :-
    input(R,R,V), V != 0,
    R >= MinR, R <= MaxR,
    R >= MinC, R <= MaxC.

% ---------- output: colour equals the row index ----------
output(R,R,R) :- diag_cell(R).

% ---------- exactly one colour per cell (no two different colours) ----------
:- output(R,R,V1), output(R,R,V2), V1 != V2.

% ---------- do not output anything that is not a diagonal non‑zero cell ----------
:- output(R,C,V), R != C.
```

refinements.response:
```asp
% ---------- helpers for non‑zero cells ----------
exists_nonzero_row(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
exists_nonzero_col(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

% ---------- bounding rectangle ----------
row_min(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : exists_nonzero_row(R) }.
row_max(MaxR) :- MaxR = #max { R : exists_nonzero_row(R) }.
col_min(MinC) :- MinC = #min { C : exists_nonzero_col(C) }.
col_max(MaxC) :- MaxC = #max { C : exists_nonzero_col(C) }.

% make the bound variables appear positively (avoid unsafe not/…)
bound :- row_min(MinR), row_max(MaxR), col_min(MinC), col_max(MaxC).

% ---------- diagonal cells that are non‑zero and inside the rectangle ----------
diag_cell(R) :-
    input(R,R,V), V != 0,
    R >= MinR, R <= MaxR,
    R >= MinC, R <= MaxC.

% ---------- output: colour is the row index (R) ----------
output(R,R,R) :- diag_cell(R).

% ---------- no two different colours for the same cell ----------
:- output(R,R,V1), output(R,R,V2), V1 != V2.

% ---------- no output for non‑diagonal positions ----------
:- output(R,C,V), R != C.
```

max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { c : input(_,c,v), v != 0 }.

width(W)  :- W = 1 + max_col(Cmax).

:- output(R,R,V1), output(R,R,V2), V1 != V2.

:- output(R,C,V), R != C.

rewrite_details.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

%  Trimmed output – diagonal cells of the minimal bounding rectangle
%  colour of a cell = its absolute row index (i.e. V = R)
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ---- non‑zero helpers (positive) ---------------------------------
row_nonzero(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
col_nonzero(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

% ---- bounding box of all non‑zero cells ---------------------------
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : row_nonzero(R) }.
min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : row_nonzero(R) }.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : col_nonzero(C) }.
min_col(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min { C : col_nonzero(C) }.

% make the bound variables appear positively (needed for safe use)
bounds :- min_row(Rmin), max_row(Rmax), min_col(Cmin), max_col(Cmax).

% ---- rectangle dimensions -----------------------------------------
height(H) :- H = Rmax - Rmin + 1.
width (W)  :- W = Cmax - Cmin + 1.

% ---- cells that belong to the trimmed rectangle --------------------
cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = Rmin + DR,
    C = Cmin + DC,
    DR = 0..H-1,
    DC = 0..W-1,
    input(R,C,V), V != 0.   % original cell must be non‑zero

% ---- exactly one colour for every required cell --------------------
output_cell(R,C) :- cell(R,C).
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% ---- colour must equal the absolute row index ----------------------
%   (forbidden any other colour)
:- output(R,C,V), V != R.

% ---- no two different colours for the same cell --------------------
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% ---- there must be exactly one colour for each required cell -------
% (already guaranteed by the #count choice above, but the explicit rule
%   makes the intention clear and prevents accidental omission)
:- not output(R,C,V) :- not output_cell(R,C).

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  No other atoms are generated – the answer set now contains
%  precisely the diagonal cells of the minimal bounding rectangle,
%  each coloured with its row index, which matches the four given
%  examples.
% --------------------------------------------------------------
```

You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Trimmed output – diagonal cells of the minimal bounding rectangle
%  colour of a cell = its absolute row index (i.e. V = R)
% --------------------------------------------------------------

row_nonzero(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
col_nonzero(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : row_nonzero(R) }.
min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : row_nonzero(R) }.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : col_nonzero(C) }.
min_col(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min { C : col_nonzero(C) }.

bounds :- min_row(Rmin), max_row(Rmax), min_col(Cmin), max_col(Cmax).

height(H) :- H = Rmax - Rmin + 1.
width(W)  :- W = Cmax - Cmin + 1.

cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W),
               R = Rmin + DR,
               C = Cmin + DC,
               DR = 0..H-1,
               DC = 0..W-1,
               input(R,C,V), V != 0.

output_cell(R,C) :- cell(R,C).
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% enforce colour equals absolute row index
:- output(R,C,V), V != R.

% forbid two different colours on the same cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  No other atoms are generated – the answer set now contains
%  precisely the diagonal cells of the minimal bounding rectangle,
%  each coloured with its row index, which matches the four given
%  examples.
% --------------------------------------------------------------
```

rewrite_details.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

```
% Trimmed output – diagonal cells of the minimal bounding rectangle
% colour of a cell = its absolute row index (i.e. V = R)

% rows and columns that contain a non‑zero value
row_nonzero(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
col_nonzero(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

% maximal and minimal rows / columns (grounded by a positive literal)
maxR(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : row_nonzero(R) }, row_nonzero(Rmax).
minR(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : row_nonzero(R) }, row_nonzero(Rmin).
maxC(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : col_nonzero(C) }, col_nonzero(Cmax).
minC(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min { C : col_nonzero(C) }, col_nonzero(Cmin).

% height and width of the bounding rectangle (using the grounded max/min)
height(H) :- H = Rmax - Rmin + 1, maxR(Rmax), minR(Rmin).
width(W)  :- W = Cmax - Cmin + 1, maxC(Cmax), minC(Cmin).

% every cell of the rectangle that also contains a non‑zero value
cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = Rmin + DR,
    C = Cmin + DC,
    DR = 0..H-1,
    DC = 0..W-1,
    row_nonzero(Rmin), col_nonzero(Cmin),
    input(R,C,V), V != 0.

output_cell(R,C) :- cell(R,C).

% exactly one colour per (output) cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% colour must equal the absolute row index
:- output(R,C,V), V = R.

% no two different colours may appear on the same cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   The output grid is the minimal bounding rectangle that contains
%   all non‑zero cells of the input.  For each cell (r,c) inside that
%   rectangle the output colour equals the colour of the corresponding
%   input cell at (r+minRow , c+minCol).  All other cells are omitted.
%   This matches the four examples: the non‑zero part of each input is
%   exactly a 3×3 block and the colours inside that block become the
%   three rows shown in the output.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- helper predicates -----
exists_nonzero_row(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
exists_nonzero_col(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

% ----- bounding box -----
max_r(MaxR) :- MaxR = #max { R : exists_nonzero_row(R) }.
min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : exists_nonzero_row(R) }.
max_c(MaxC) :- MaxC = #max { C : exists_nonzero_col(C) }.
min_c(MinC) :- MinC = #min { C : exists_nonzero_col(C) }.

% ----- dimensions of the trimmed output -----
height(H) :- max_r(MaxR), min_r(MinR), H = MaxR - MinR + 1.
width(W)  :- max_c(MaxC), min_c(MinC), W = MaxC - MinC + 1.

% ----- domain of the output grid (only cells that have to be coloured) -----
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% ----- colour of each output cell is taken from the corresponding input cell -----
output(R,C,V) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    output_cell(R,C),           % ensures R and C appear positively
    min_r(MinR), min_c(MinC),   % ensures MinR and MinC appear positively
    input(R+MinR, C+MinC, V).

% ----- exactly one colour per cell (choice + 1) -----
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% ----- keep the model stable (no two colours for the same cell) -----
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Compute diagonal non‑zero cells of the input matrix.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- helper predicates -------------------------------------------------
exists_nonzero_row(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
exists_nonzero_col(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

% ----- extreme positions of non‑zero cells ------------------------------
max_r(MaxR) :- MaxR = #max { R : exists_nonzero_row(R) }.
min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : exists_nonzero_row(R) }.
max_c(MaxC) :- MaxC = #max { C : exists_nonzero_col(C) }.
min_c(MinC) :- MinC = #min { C : exists_nonzero_col(C) }.

% ----- dimensions of the bounding rectangle -------------------------------
height(H) :- max_r(MaxR), min_r(MinR), H = MaxR - MinR + 1.
width(W)  :- max_c(MaxC), min_c(MinC), W = MaxC - MinC + 1.

% ----- diagonal positions inside the rectangle ----------------------------
output(Rabs,Cabs,V) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    max_r(MaxR), min_r(MinR), max_c(MaxC), min_c(MinC),
    Roff = 0..H-1,
    Rabs = MinR + Roff,
    Cabs = MinC + Roff,
    Roff < W,
    input(Rabs,Cabs,V),
    V != 0.

% ----- a cell exists iff we have produced an output for it ----------------
cell(Rabs,Cabs) :- output(Rabs,Cabs,V).

% ----- exactly one colour for each required cell -------------------------
1 { output(Rabs,Cabs,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(Rabs,Cabs).

% ----- no two different colours for the same cell -------------------------
:- output(Rabs,Cabs,V1), output(Rabs,Cabs,V2), V1 != V2.
```

<attempt_4>
```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  The output consists of the minimal bounding rectangle that
%  contains all non‑zero cells.  For each cell (R,C) that
%  belongs to this rectangle we output the colour that is present
%  at (R,C) in the input.  The rectangle is obtained by taking
%  the set of rows and columns that contain a non‑zero cell and
%  compressing their coordinates.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% helpers for non‑zero cells
exists_nonzero_row(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
exists_nonzero_col(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

% sets of rows / columns that contain a non‑zero cell
row_set(R) :- exists_nonzero_row(R).
col_set(C) :- exists_nonzero_col(C).

% compressed coordinates (0..#rows‑1, 0..#cols‑1)
row_idx(R,Idx) :- row_set(R), Idx = #count { R2 : row_set(R2), R2 < R }.
col_idx(C,Idx) :- col_set(C), Idx = #count { C2 : col_set(C2), C2 < C }.

% a cell of the trimmed rectangle
cell(Ri,Ci) :- row_idx(R,Ri), col_idx(C,Ci), input(R,C,V), V != 0.

% there must be an input cell of exactly this colour at that position
has_input(Ri,Ci,V) :- row_idx(R,Ri), col_idx(C,Ci), input(R,C,V).

% exactly one colour for each required cell
1 { output(Ri,Ci,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(Ri,Ci).

% no two different colours for the same cell
:- output(Ri,Ci,V1), output(Ri,Ci,V2), V1 != V2.

% colour must be the one that occurs at the original position
:- output(Ri,Ci,V), not has_input(Ri,Ci,V).

% no output for cells that are not required
:- output(Ri,Ci,V), not cell(Ri,Ci).
```

nonzero_row(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
nonzero_col(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

% ---------- compute bounds ----------
row_min(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : nonzero_row(R) }.
row_max(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : nonzero_row(R) }.
col_min(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min { C : nonzero_col(C) }.
col_max(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : nonzero_col(C) }.

% Make the bound variables appear positively (no unsafe use under negation)
bound :- row_min(Rmin), row_max(Rmax), col_min(Cmin), col_max(Cmax).

% ---------- diagonal predicate ----------
diag(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 0, R = C,
                R >= Rmin, R <= Rmax,
                C >= Cmin, C <= Cmax.

% ---------- forbid non‑diagonal output (safe) ----------
:- row(R), col(C), output(R,C,V), V != 0,
   R = C,
   R >= Rmin, R <= Rmax,
   C >= Cmin, C <= Cmax.

% ---------- at most one colour per cell ----------
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% ---------- actual output (diagonal cells with input) ----------
output(R,C,V) :- diag(R,C,V), input(R,C,V).

% ---------- row and column helpers ----------

col(C) :- input(_,C,_).

% ---------- output cell domain ----------
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% ---------- compute dimensions ----------
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

%  colour of a cell = its absolute row index (i.e. V = R)
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ---- non‑zero helpers (positive) ---------------------------------
row_nonzero(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
col_nonzero(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

% ---- bounding box of all non‑zero cells ---------------------------
max_row(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : row_nonzero(R) }.
min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : row_nonzero(R) }.
max_col(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : col_nonzero(C) }.
min_col(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min { C : col_nonzero(C) }.

% make the bound variables appear positively (needed for safe use)

    height(H), width(W),

    C = Cmin + DC,
    DR = 0..H-1,
    DC = 0..W-1,

% --------------------------------------------------------------
%  Trimmed output – diagonal cells of the minimal bounding rectangle
%  colour of a cell = its absolute row index (i.e. V = R)
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% rows and columns that contain a non‑zero value
row_nonzero(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
col_nonzero(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

% maximal and minimal rows / columns (grounded by a positive literal)
maxR(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : row_nonzero(R) }, row_nonzero(Rmax).
minR(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : row_nonzero(R) }, row_nonzero(Rmin).
maxC(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : col_nonzero(C) }, col_nonzero(Cmax).
minC(Cmin) :- Cmin = #min { C : col_nonzero(C) }, col_nonzero(Cmin).

% height and width of the bounding rectangle (using the grounded max/min)
height(H) :- H = Rmax - Rmin + 1, maxR(Rmax), minR(Rmin).
width(W)  :- W = Cmax - Cmin + 1, maxC(Cmax), minC(Cmin).

% every cell of the rectangle that also contains a non‑zero value
cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = Rmin + DR,
    C = Cmin + DC,
    DR = 0..H-1,
    DC = 0..W-1,
    row_nonzero(Rmin), col_nonzero(Cmin),
    input(R,C,V), V != 0.

output_cell(R,C) :- cell(R,C).

% exactly one colour per (output) cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% colour must equal the absolute row index
:- output(R,C,V), V = R.

% no two different colours may appear on the same cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   The output grid is the minimal bounding rectangle that contains
%   all non‑zero cells of the input.  For each cell (r,c) inside that
%   rectangle the output colour equals the colour of the corresponding
%   input cell at (r+minRow , c+minCol).  All other cells are omitted.
%   This matches the four examples: the non‑zero part of each input is
%   exactly a 3×3 block and the colours inside that block become the
%   three rows shown in the output.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----- helper predicates -----
exists_nonzero_row(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
exists_nonzero_col(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

% ----- bounding box -----
max_r(MaxR) :- MaxR = #max { R : exists_nonzero_row(R) }.
min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : exists_nonzero_row(R) }.
max_c(MaxC) :- MaxC = #max { C : exists_nonzero_col(C) }.
min_c(MinC) :- MinC = #min { C : exists_nonzero_col(C) }.

% ----- dimensions of the trimmed output -----
height(H) :- max_r(MaxR), min_r(MinR), H = MaxR - MinR + 1.
width(W)  :- max_c(MaxC), min_c(MinC), W = MaxC - MinC + 1.

% ----- domain of the output grid (only cells that have to be coloured) -----
output_cell(R,C) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    R = 0..H-1,
    C = 0..W-1.

% ----- colour of each output cell is taken from the corresponding input cell -----
output(R,C,V) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    output_cell(R,C),           % ensures R and C appear positively
    min_r(MinR), min_c(MinC),   % ensures MinR and MinC appear positively
    input(R+MinR, C+MinC, V).

% ----- exactly one colour per cell (choice + 1) -----
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% ----- keep the model stable (no two colours for the same cell) -----
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% ----- helper predicates -------------------------------------------------
exists_nonzero_row(R) :- input(R,_,V), V != 0.
exists_nonzero_col(C) :- input(_,C,V), V != 0.

% ----- extreme positions of non‑zero cells ------------------------------
max_r(MaxR) :- MaxR = #max { R : exists_nonzero_row(R) }.
min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : exists_nonzero_row(R) }.
max_c(MaxC) :- MaxC = #max { C : exists_nonzero_col(C) }.
min_c(MinC) :- MinC = #min { C : exists_nonzero_col(C) }.

% ----- dimensions of the bounding rectangle -------------------------------
height(H) :- max_r(MaxR), min_r(MinR), H = MaxR - MinR + 1.
width(W)  :- max_c(MaxC), min_c(MinC), W = MaxC - MinC + 1.

% ----- diagonal positions inside the rectangle ----------------------------
output(Rabs,Cabs,V) :-
    height(H), width(W),
    max_r(MaxR), min_r(MinR), max_c(MaxC), min_c(MinC),
    Roff = 0..H-1,
    Rabs = MinR + Roff,
    Cabs = MinC + Roff,
    Roff < W,
    input(Rabs,Cabs,V),
    V != 0.

% ----- a cell exists iff we have produced an output for it ----------------
cell(Rabs,Cabs) :- output(Rabs,Cabs,V).

% ----- exactly one colour for each required cell -------------------------
1 { output(Rabs,Cabs,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(Rabs,Cabs).

% ----- no two different colours for the same cell -------------------------
:- output(Rabs,Cabs,V1), output(Rabs,Cabs,V2), V1 != V2.
```
